result:
I keep thinking about the small brass gear I found in the pocket of a second-hand coat I bought in Reykjavík.  It is no larger than a fingernail, twenty-one teeth, three spokes, the color of burnt honey when you hold it to the light.  There is no watch in the world tiny enough to need a gear this small, no clock that would notice its absence.  And yet someone machined it, measured it, polished it, placed it where it could turn in concert with other hidden cogs.  I carried it across three countries and through four apartments, and every time I emptied a drawer I expected it to be gone, but it is still here on my desk, stubbornly present, like a syllable in a lost language that refuses to forget its accent.  When I spin it on the marble desktop it hums a note between F and F-sharp, a frequency that makes the neighbor’s terrier bark through the wall.  I have begun to suspect the gear is not a gear at all, but a seed.  Somewhere a machine-tree is waiting for me to plant it.  When I die the executor of my estate will hold it up to the light, shrug, and drop it in the trash.  The gear does not mind.  It has already begun to calcify my pockets into a soft ore it can feed on.  Time works in reverse for objects too small to be loved.

I was born in the intermission of a thunderstorm.  My mother says the obstetrician hummed “Stormy Weather” while he cut the cord, and that the lights flickered twice, once when I inhaled, once when I screamed.  For years I believed this meant I contained two souls: one that gathers electricity, one that spends it.  I spent the first soul on ordinary things—lighting cigarettes I never smoked, shocking supermarket cashiers, powering the cheap plastic radio I kept under my pillow so I could fall asleep to foreign voices.  The second soul I saved for something larger, though I did not know what.  At nineteen I fell in love with a woman who collected lightning-struck wood; she stacked it like bones on her balcony, claiming each piece still held a charge you could measure with your tongue.  We would sit out there during summer monsoons, drinking cane alcohol mixed with powdered mango, waiting for the sky to choose us.  One night it did.  The bolt hit the neighbor’s TV antenna, traveled down the galvanized pipe, leapt across two meters of wet air, and entered her left shoulder.  I was holding her right hand.  The flash was colorless, louder than white.  When I could see again she was laughing, hair standing away from her scalp like a dandelion clock.  She said, “That was the first honest thing that ever happened to me.”  Six months later she left to chase a storm system across the Bay of Bengal.  She mails me postcards whenever the season begins: a rectangle of corrugated cardboard, no message, just a scorch mark in the shape of a continent.  I keep them in a biscuit tin.  They still smell like ozone and mango.

I have never learned to swim.  My parents enrolled me in lessons at the municipal pool where the water tasted of copper and the instructor wore a whistle shaped like a fish.  Every Tuesday I stood on the tiles clutching a Styrofoam kickboard painted like Mr. Incredible, staring at the blue rectangle as if it were a hole in the world.  The other children bobbed, squealed, swallowed chlorinated joy.  I could not convince my lungs that air might be optional.  One afternoon the instructor, impatient with my tears, pulled me into the deep end by the strap of my trunks.  The water closed over my ears with a sound like a library closing.  I saw the skylights refract into liquid cathedrals.  My feet found nothing.  In that panic I felt the second soul uncurl, the one I had been saving.  It did not save me; instead it recorded the moment, filed it under “evidence against drowning.”  What saved me was the janitor, an elderly man with a mustache like pushed-up snow, who dove in fully clothed and carried me out as if I were a newspaper he planned to read later.  He wrapped me in a towel that smelled of industrial bleach and whispered, “Remember, the water is more afraid of you.”  I have never verified this claim, but I keep it folded in the same pocket as the brass gear.  Sometimes, on flights when the cabin pressure drops, I take the gear out and press it against the window.  The clouds look solid enough to walk on if you are willing to ignore the physics.

When I turned thirty I decided to walk every street in the city where I lived.  Not the suburbs, not the highways, just the grid inside the river’s bend.  I bought a cheap pedometer that clicked like a Geiger counter and began early on a Sunday in March when the frost still held the shadows in its mouth.  I walked alone, no headphones, no map except the one I was rewriting with each step.  The city revealed itself as a palimpsest: underneath the bakeries and bike lanes I found the bones of an older city—cobblestones poking through asphalt, iron rings where horses once waited, a faded advertisement for lemon soap painted on brick so soft it could be brushed away with a thumb.  I talked to no one except dogs tied outside cafés; they offered concise reviews of the weather and the human condition.  By the end of the first month my shoes had memorized the angle of every curb.  I began to notice anomalies: a street that appeared on Tuesday but was gone on Thursday, a mailbox that received letters addressed to people I almost remembered being.  I plotted these irregularities on a blank sheet of vellum, connecting them with red thread until the map resembled a heart fibrillating.  One dusk I turned a corner I had turned a hundred times and found a shop called “While You Wait.”  The window displayed clocks without hands, jars of dust labeled by decade, a single baby shoe bronzed like a trophy.  Inside, an old woman sat knitting with needles made of street signs.  She did not look up.  I asked her what she sold.  “Time,” she said, “but only the parts you waste.”  I laughed, because that sounded like something I would write on a postcard to myself.  When I left the shop was gone; in its place stood a tattoo parlor offering “regret removal.”  I walked home counting my pulse instead of steps.  The pedometer had reset to 00:00, but the clicking continued inside my chest.

Years later, in a different city, I attended a conference on obsolete futures.  The keynote speaker wore a suit the color of cathode-ray static and spoke about the longevity of abandoned websites.  He projected a slide of GeoCities neighborhoods flickering on a server in a decommissioned salt mine.  “Digital ghosts,” he called them, “still hosting potluck recipes and under-construction gifs, waiting for browsers that no longer exist.”  During the coffee break I found myself beside him at the urn.  I asked whether he believed objects could haunt us in the same way.  He stirred his cup with a plastic stick shaped like an arrow and said, “Only if we forget to forget them.”  I thought of the brass gear, ticking softly against my thigh like a second heartbeat.  I offered to show it to him, but when I reached into my pocket I found only a slip of paper printed with the URL of a guestbook last signed in 1997: “Cool page, come visit my webring.”  The speaker nodded as if this proved his thesis.  Later that night I typed the URL into an emulator running Netscape Navigator.  The background was black with lime-green text that read, “You are visitor ████.”  The counter overflowed the frame, digits spilling like ants across the monitor.  I left a message: “I was looking for a gear.”  A week later the site replied with an auto-generated email containing a single line: “Seeds grow best in darkness.”  I printed the message, folded it into a paper boat, and set it afloat in the fountain outside the library.  It dissolved before it reached the drain, leaving behind a film of letters that spelled the name of the city where I first lost my fear of drowning.

I used to believe that if I kept moving I could stay ahead of my own reflection.  I changed apartments the way other people change toothbrushes, leaving behind closets full of half-used spices and shower curtains molded into accidental Rorschach tests.  In every new mirror the face arrived later, as if it had to commute from a previous address.  I greeted it with the enthusiasm reserved for Jehovah’s Witnesses.  One winter the face refused to show up at all.  I stood in the bathroom of a fifth-floor walk-up, fogging the glass with vodka breath, waiting.  The absence was not a blank space; it was an entity wearing camouflage made of my own expectations.  I traced the outline where my cheek ought to have been and felt the mirror warm under my finger, as if the glass were blushing.  I understood then that I had been subtracted, digit by digit, like a lottery played in reverse.  I opened the medicine cabinet and found the brass gear sitting on the middle shelf, balanced on a tube of travel toothpaste.  It had grown: now the size of a coaster, teeth like shark fins.  I tried to pick it up but it rotated just beyond my grip, as if attached to an invisible lathe.  I asked it what it wanted.  It answered by accelerating until it became a blur of bronze, then a sphere, then a sun.  The bathroom filled with the smell of hot coins.  When the light subsided the mirror had cracked into a mosaic; in each shard I saw a younger version of myself, barefoot on the municipal pool’s edge, hesitating.  They all jumped at once.  The sound was like a sigh released after decades of held breath.  My reflection returned, older, wetter, but entire.  The gear was gone; in its place lay a single drop of mercury trembling like a guilty secret.  I scooped it into an empty film canister and capped it.  Some nights I hold the canister to my ear and hear the ocean reciting my name in Morse code.

There is a 24-hour laundromat three blocks from where I now live that contains a black hole.  I discovered it at 3:12 a.m. when I went to wash the shirt I had worn to a funeral I was not invited to.  The machines were singing in minor keys, a hymn for lost socks.  In the back corner, behind the dryers that eat quarters like communion wafers, the tiled floor curved downward, a gentle depression no larger than a dinner plate.  I knelt and felt the fabric of space give slightly, like a mattress with a bad spring.  A sign handwritten in dry-erase marker read: “Do not tumble dry.”  I dropped a penny into the dark.  It did not echo.  I returned every week with a single item that needed cleansing: a scarf that smelled of gunpowder, a pillowcase printed with faded roses, the tie I wore when I lied about loving her.  Each time the hole had widened by the diameter of a coin.  I began to schedule my visits around lunar phases.  The owner, a woman named Luz who wore her name on a gold chain, pretended not to notice.  She sat at the counter reading romance novels in languages she could not speak, turning pages as if they were tarot cards.  One night I asked her what would happen when the hole swallowed the laundromat.  She closed her book and said, “Then we will finally get rid of the stains we were saving for god.”  I thought of the mercury in the film canister, wondered if it would orbit the singularity forever.  Last month the hole reached the change machine; instead of bills it now dispenses small metal tokens stamped with the year of your birth.  Mine reads 1991, which is incorrect, but I spend them anyway.  They come out warm, as if freshly cried.

I keep a list of places where I have hidden pieces of myself.  Item #47: inside a piano at the Copenhagen train station, between the C and C-sharp keys that are never played simultaneously.  Item #23: under the false bottom of a birdhouse in the botanical garden, wrapped in a page torn from a Swedish Bible—Revelation, the part about the silence in heaven.  Item #08: in the battery compartment of a Geiger counter at a flea market in Kraków, where a woman sold crockery chipped into maps.  I do not remember what each fragment contains; that is the point.  Someday I will be reassembled by a stranger who thinks he is buying antiques.  He will hold up a shard that tastes of copper and pool water and wonder why his pulse syncs to its rhythm.  Perhaps he will plant it by accident, and a machine-tree will grow whose fruit is clocks.  Crows will learn to tell time incorrectly, and the world will run a few minutes late, long enough for everyone to say the things they meant to withhold.  I will be there in the hush between the second hand stuttering, apologizing for the delay.

The brass gear returned last night.  I woke to find it on my chest, weightless as a fever dream.  It had shrunk again, back to the size of a baby tooth.  Around its edge ran an inscription in a script that looked like the skyline of a city after an earthquake.  I placed it under my pillow and dreamed of a library where all the books were written in the language of mechanical birds.  When I opened them they fluttered, scattering gear-shaped seeds across the floor.  The librarian, who wore my face at age seven, shushed me with a finger made of clock hands.  She said, “You are overdue.”  I offered her the mercury, but she only wanted the sound of my mother humming during thunderstorms.  I sang it as best I could.  The seeds sprouted into vines that spelled the name of every street I ever walked.  At the end of the dream I was back at the municipal pool, standing on the edge.  The water had been replaced by mirror shards reflecting clouds that looked like corrected mistakes.  I jumped.  This time I did not sink; I folded, again and again, until I was the size of the brass gear.  A hand—Luz’s, the janitor’s, perhaps my own—picked me up and placed me in a pocket.  I could hear the ocean translating itself into footsteps.  It sounded like coming home.

Outside, dawn arrives the way a page loads on dial-up: line by line, grainy, miraculous.  I open the window.  The city inhales.  Somewhere a laundromat collapses politely into itself, taking the stains of a thousand strangers with it.  Somewhere a woman on a boat in the Bay of Bengal licks a postcard and tastes my childhood.  Somewhere a 24-hour clock loses its hands and keeps ticking anyway.  I take the gear from under the pillow and hold it to the light.  It is no longer brass; it has become translucent, a slice of early morning sky solidified by accident.  Inside, a tiny version of me walks across a bridge that is also a sentence I started writing decades ago and never finished.  He pauses in the middle, looks up, waves.  I wave back.  The bridge trembles, a harmonic between F and F-sharp.  The terrier next door barks once, twice.  I place the gear on the windowsill.  It begins to turn, very slowly, powered by the difference between what I remember and what actually happened.  I sit down to watch.  There is no hurry.  Time, like water, is only afraid when it is still.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
